sections.summary: Document title only: Codex Standard for Avocado (CXS 197-1995); heading, not substantive.

sections.section: Adopted in 1995. Amended in 2015. Revised in 2013.
sections.summary: Document history: Adopted in 1995, revised in 2013 and amended in 2015.

Scope: This Standard applies to commercial varieties of avocado (Persea americana Mill., Lauraceae) supplied fresh after preparation and packaging, and excludes parthenocarpic fruit and avocados intended for industrial processing.

Quality provisions: avocados must meet minimum requirements for soundness, cleanliness, freedom from pests, abnormal moisture, foreign odours/tastes and temperature damage, have a stalk ≤10 mm (absence allowed if attachment dry/whole), be physiologically mature to complete ripening and withstand transport; maturity dry‑matter thresholds are specified by variety and avocados are classified into Extra, Class I and Class II with increasing allowances for superficial defects (skin defect limits given by class).

Minimum requirements: avocados must be whole, sound (no rot), clean, practically free of pests and temperature damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign smell/taste, have a stalk cut to ≤10 mm (absence permitted if attachment is dry and whole), be physiologically mature to complete ripening and be capable of withstanding transport and arriving in satisfactory condition, with maturity dry‑matter minimums set by variety (see 2.1.2).

sections.section: 2.1.1 The avocados must have reached a stage of physiological development which will ensure the completion of the ripening process, in accordance with criteria proper to the variety and to the area in which they are grown. The mature fruit should be free of bitterness.
sections.summary: Physiological development and transport: avocados must be sufficiently mature (per variety and growing area) to complete ripening and be free of bitterness, and must be developed and conditioned to withstand handling and arrive in satisfactory condition at destination.

Maturity (dry matter): minimum dry matter at harvest measured by drying to constant weight — 21% for Hass and 20% for Torres, Fuerte, Pinkerton, Edranol and Reed; other varieties (e.g., Antillean/West Indian/Guatemalan) may have lower dry matter.

Classification: avocados are classified into three classes (Extra, Class I and Class II) with Extra being superior and virtually defect‑free, Class I allowing slight superficial defects (limited skin defect area), and Class II allowing more pronounced defects while still meeting minimum requirements.

Extra class: avocados must be of superior quality and characteristic of the variety, free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation, and the stalk, if present, must be intact (requirement applies to a fruit lot, not individual fruits).

Class I: avocados must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety; slight defects in shape/colour and slight skin defects (corkiness, healed lenticels) and sunburn are allowed provided they do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation (maximum total skin defect area 4 cm²) and defects must not affect the flesh; stalk, if present, may be slightly damaged.

Class II: includes avocados meeting minimum requirements but not higher classes; defects in shape/colour and skin defects (corkiness, healed lenticels) and sunburn are allowed provided essential characteristics are retained (maximum total skin defect area 6 cm²), defects must not affect the flesh, and the stalk, if present, may be damaged.

Sizing: avocados may be sized by weight using defined size codes with specified gram ranges (and a 170 g minimum for Antillean/West Indian/Guatemalan and other unspecified varieties), or by count provided the smallest fruit in a package weighs at least 75% of the largest to ensure size uniformity.

Tolerances: quality tolerances permit a percentage of fruit in a package not meeting the stated class (Extra 5%, Class I 10% with up to 1% decay allowed, Class II 10% with decay ≤2%), and size tolerances permit 10% by number or weight of fruit to be of the size immediately above or below that indicated on the package.

Quality tolerances (summary): Extra packages may contain up to 5% by number/weight of fruit not meeting Extra but meeting Class I (or within its tolerances); Class I may contain up to 10% not meeting Class I but meeting Class II (including up to 1% decay); Class II may contain up to 10% not meeting the class or the minimum requirements (decay ≤2%).
4.1.1 'Extra' Class tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of avocados in an Extra package may fail to meet Extra but must meet Class I or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.

4.1.2 Class I tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of avocados in a Class I package may fail to meet Class I but must meet Class II or, exceptionally, be within its tolerances, and up to 1% may be affected by decay.

4.1.3 Class II tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of avocados in a Class II package may fail to meet the class or the minimum requirements, except that avocados affected by decay must not exceed 2%.
4.2 Size tolerances: for all classes, 10% by number or weight of avocados in a package may correspond to the size immediately above or below the size indicated on the package.

Presentation (uniformity and packaging): packages must contain uniform contents of the same origin, variety, quality and size with the visible portion representative of the whole; packaging materials must be new, clean, non‑damaging and printed/labelled with non‑toxic ink/glue, containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements and packing must comply with the Code of Practice CXC 44‑1995.
Uniformity: each package must be uniform and contain only avocados of the same origin, variety, quality and size, and the visible portion must be representative of the entire contents.

Packaging requirements for avocados: containers must protect the produce; internal materials must be new, clean and of a quality that avoids external or internal damage (including recycled food‑grade material), paper or stamps are allowed if printed/labelled with non‑toxic ink or glue, and packing must comply with the Code of Practice for the Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44‑1995).

Containers for avocados must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance characteristics to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation, and packages must be free of foreign matter and odours.

Sets labelling/marking requirements for consumer and non‑retail avocado packages supplementing the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985): produce name (and optionally variety) when not visible and specified particulars for non‑retail containers must be legible, indelible and grouped on the same side or provided in accompanying documentation (with bulk shipments documented).
Introductory provision noting that consumer package labelling must meet the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985) and additional specific provisions follow.
If produce is not visible from the outside, consumer packages must be labelled with the name of the produce and may include the variety name.

Non‑retail containers must bear specified particulars legibly and indelibly on the same side and visible from outside or have those particulars in accompanying documents, with bulk consignments' particulars on shipment documents; recycled food‑grade materials are acceptable for packaging.
Identification labelling must include the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, with an optional identification code.
For non‑retail containers, the name of the produce must be provided if contents are not visible and the variety name may be included optionally.
Origin labelling must state the country of origin and may optionally include the district, national, regional or local place name where grown.
Commercial identification must indicate class, size expressed as minimum and maximum weight in grams or by count, and may optionally state net weight.
Provides for an optional official inspection mark on packaging.
Requires the produce to comply with Codex maximum limits for contaminants and toxins (CXS 193‑1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends that produce be prepared and handled in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53‑2003) and related Codex texts, and states the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21‑1997.
